codeReview.summary: Solid implementation of ACPProfile data model and ACPProfileStore. The model is well-structured with proper Codable/Identifiable/Equatable conformance, and the store provides complete CRUD operations backed by UserDefaults. 23 tests cover model construction, serialization, all permission policies, store CRUD, persistence, and edge cases. The DiscoveryView fix for the missing remoteCommand parameter was a necessary build fix. The doc comment about 'integrate with the Rust layer' is slightly overstated — the integration is indirect (via AgentPermissionPolicy UniFFI type), not a direct sync mechanism. This is acceptable for the current scope but worth noting.

issues.description: Doc comment says 'They integrate with the Rust layer through the AgentPermissionPolicy UniFFI type for cross-platform permission consistency' — this is technically true (AgentPermissionPolicy is a UniFFI type) but the feature description also says 'Store syncs with Rust layer to maintain cross-platform consistency'. There is no actual Rust store sync in this implementation. ACPProfileStore is purely iOS-side with UserDefaults persistence. The Rust AcpProfile struct exists in detection.rs but has no bridge to the Swift store. This is not a bug — the profiles are platform-specific user preferences — but the description overclaims.

The persist() method silently swallows JSON encoding errors with `try?`. If encoding fails (e.g., due to a non-encodable field added in the future), the user's profiles would be silently lost. This is consistent with the project's patterns but could be improved with logging in a future pass.

sharedStateObservations.observation: ACPProfileStore uses UserDefaults for persistence with a key pattern 'litter.acpProfiles'. The mission's AGENTS.md documents coding conventions but does not mention the UserDefaults key naming convention. Other stores in the codebase use similar patterns (AgentSelectionStore uses 'litter.selectedAgent', 'litter.serverAgentTypes'). A shared convention for UserDefaults key naming would prevent conflicts.

AgentPermissionPolicy is made retroactively Codable in AgentPickerView.swift (not in the model file or a shared extension file). UniFFI-generated types that need platform conformance (Codable, Identifiable) are being extended in view files. This pattern is repeated for both AgentType and AgentPermissionPolicy in AgentPickerView.swift. A dedicated extensions file (e.g., Models/UniFFIExtensions.swift) would be cleaner, but this is consistent with how the project currently does it.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: AgentPickerView.swift:41 — 'extension AgentPermissionPolicy: @retroactive Codable'; AgentPickerView.swift:15 — 'extension AgentType: @retroactive Codable'

sharedStateObservations.observation: The platform-ui-worker skill focuses on DiscoveryView modifications and agent type threading, but this feature (ACPProfile model/store) was a pure model + persistence task that didn't touch DiscoveryView except for an unrelated build fix. The skill was followed but its procedure doesn't cover model/store creation patterns. This isn't a deviation — the worker correctly adapted — but the skill scope could note that model features may be assigned to this worker.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: Skill SKILL.md describes 'iOS SwiftUI views for agent selection connection wiring' and 'UniFFI binding consumption' but feature was about creating a data model + persistence store with full test coverage.

summary: PASS — ACPProfile and ACPProfileStore are well-implemented with proper Swift conventions (Codable, Identifiable, Equatable, @MainActor). 23 unit tests cover model construction, all three permission policy round-trips, full CRUD lifecycle, persistence across store re-reads, and edge cases. Build verified via make ios-sim-fast and all 97 tests pass. Two non-blocking issues: the doc comment slightly overclaims Rust integration (the store is purely iOS-side UserDefaults), and persist() silently swallows encoding errors. The DiscoveryView fix for the missing remoteCommand parameter was a necessary pre-existing build break fix.
